label: German 3rd Army — East Prussia to Gdańsk Corridor
notes: German 3rd Army invaded from East Prussia, cutting off the Polish Corridor and isolating Danzig.

label: German Army Group South — Silesia into Poland
notes: Germany's Army Group South (1 million troops) invaded from Silesia driving toward Warsaw and the Bug River.

label: Soviet Invasion of Eastern Poland
notes: Red Army invaded eastern Poland on September 17, per secret Molotov–Ribbentrop protocol, meeting German forces at the Bug River line.

label: German Panzer Drive Through Ardennes (May 1940)
notes: German Army Group A's 7 Panzer divisions drove through the supposedly impassable Ardennes, bypassing the Maginot Line and enveloping Allied forces in Belgium.

label: German Panzer Dash to English Channel
notes: Guderian's Panzers raced 400 km to the English Channel coast in 10 days, cutting off Allied armies in Belgium. 'Sickle Cut' (Sichelschnitt) maneuver.

label: Dunkirk Evacuation — Channel Crossing
notes: 338,226 Allied troops evacuated across the English Channel to England over nine days by Royal Navy and civilian vessels.

label: Army Group North — Baltic States to Leningrad
notes: German Army Group North drove 750 km through the Baltic states to besiege Leningrad in just 79 days.

label: Army Group Centre — Minsk to Moscow
notes: Germany's strongest army group drove from the Polish border to the outskirts of Moscow (within 20 km) before being halted in December 1941.

label: Army Group South — Ukraine and Kyiv
notes: Army Group South drove through Ukraine, encircling 665,000 Soviet troops at Kyiv in September 1941 — history's largest military encirclement.

label: German Drive to Caucasus Oil Fields (1942)
notes: Germany's 1942 Summer Offensive (Case Blue) aimed to seize Soviet oil in the Caucasus. Army Group A reached the northern Caucasus foothills before being halted and withdrawing.

label: Operation Uranus — Northern Pincer
notes: Soviet Southwestern Front broke through Romanian 3rd Army north of Stalingrad, driving south to link with forces from the south at Kalach.

label: Operation Uranus — Southern Pincer
notes: Soviet Stalingrad Front smashed through Romanian 4th Army south of Stalingrad, closing the ring on the German 6th Army at Kalach on November 23.

label: Rommel's Afrika Korps — Tobruk to El Alamein
notes: After capturing Tobruk, Rommel advanced 300 km to El Alamein — the high-water mark of German-Italian expansion in North Africa — before being stopped in July 1942.

label: British 8th Army — El Alamein to Tunisia
notes: After El Alamein, Montgomery's Eighth Army pursued Rommel 2,200 km across Libya to Tunisia in the final North African campaign.

label: Operation Torch — Algeria to Tunisia
notes: US and British forces landed in Algeria and advanced east toward Tunisia, squeezing Axis forces between Allied armies from east and west.

label: Allied Invasion of Sicily (Husky)
notes: British 8th Army landed on Sicily's southeastern coast; US 7th Army on the south coast. Combined forces captured the island within 38 days.

label: Allied Advance — Salerno to Rome
notes: US 5th Army landed at Salerno (Operation Avalanche) and fought northward to Rome, captured June 4, 1944, against strong German defensive lines (Gustav Line).

label: D-Day Assault — Omaha Beach
notes: US Navy and Coast Guard landing craft carried US 1st and 29th Infantry Divisions from staging areas off Portsmouth to Omaha Beach under heavy fire.

label: Allied Breakout — Normandy to Paris
notes: Operation Cobra (July 25) broke through German lines; Patton's 3rd Army raced east. Paris liberated August 25, 1944 — 80 days after D-Day.

label: Allied Advance — Paris to the Rhine
notes: Allied armies raced from liberated Paris to the Rhine through the autumn of 1944, covering 700 km in five weeks before German resistance stiffened near the Siegfried Line.

label: German Ardennes Offensive (Battle of the Bulge)
notes: Germany's last major Western offensive drove 85 km into Allied lines through the Ardennes, creating the 'Bulge.' Halted by Allied resistance at Bastogne and Patton's relief drive.

label: Soviet Vistula-Oder Offensive
notes: Soviet forces under Zhukov and Konev advanced 500 km from Warsaw to the Oder River (60 km from Berlin) in just three weeks — the fastest major advance of the war.

label: Allied Rhine Crossing — Remagen to Central Germany
notes: After seizing the Remagen bridge, Allied forces crossed the Rhine and drove into central Germany, linking with Soviet forces at the Elbe River on April 25, 1945.

label: Soviet Final Assault on Berlin
notes: Zhukov's 1st Belorussian Front and Konev's 1st Ukrainian Front encircled and assaulted Berlin with 2.5 million troops, ending the war in Europe.

label: Japanese Carrier Strike Force Route — Hittokapu Bay to Launch Point
notes: Japanese Kido Butai (6 carriers) sailed from Hittokapu Bay, Kurile Islands southward in radio silence to their launch point northeast of Hawaii, launching 353 aircraft against Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941.

label: Japanese Expansion — Malaya to Burma (1942)
notes: After capturing Singapore, Japanese forces advanced into Burma, threatening India and cutting the Burma Road supply route to China.

label: US Central Pacific Island-Hopping — Tarawa to Marianas
notes: Nimitz's Central Pacific campaign bypassed heavily defended Japanese islands, capturing Tarawa, Kwajalein, Eniwetok, and the Marianas (Saipan, Guam, Tinian) to bring B-29s within range of Japan.

label: MacArthur's SW Pacific Campaign — Guadalcanal to Philippines
notes: MacArthur's Southwest Pacific Area Command advanced from Guadalcanal through New Guinea to the Philippines, fulfilling his promise to return and liberating Manila by March 1945.

label: B-29 Strategic Bombing of Japan — Marianas to Tokyo
notes: B-29 Superfortresses flying from Saipan, Tinian, and Guam conducted systematic bombing of Japanese cities from November 1944, culminating in the Tokyo firestorm raid of March 9–10, 1945.

label: Enola Gay — Tinian to Hiroshima
notes: B-29 Enola Gay departed North Field, Tinian Island at 2:45 AM on August 6, 1945, and dropped 'Little Boy' on Hiroshima at 8:15 AM local time.

label: Bockscar — Tinian to Nagasaki
notes: B-29 Bockscar departed Tinian on August 9, 1945 targeting Kokura (obscured by clouds) before diverting to Nagasaki and dropping 'Fat Man' at 11:02 AM local time.

label: Soviet August Storm — Manchuria Invasion
notes: Soviet Far East forces launched Operation August Storm, invading Manchuria along three axes simultaneously with 1.5 million troops, shattering the Japanese Kwantung Army in days.

label: Germany Invades Yugoslavia and Greece (April 1941)
notes: After Yugoslavia rejected Axis membership, Germany invaded alongside Hungary, Italy, and Bulgaria. Yugoslavia surrendered in 11 days; Greece fell by April 27.

label: German Airborne Assault on Crete
notes: Operation Mercury: 22,000 German paratroopers and glider troops assaulted Crete. The first major airborne invasion in history succeeded, but at such cost that Germany never mounted another major airborne operation.

label: Afrika Korps Arrival — Tripoli to Libya
notes: Rommel's Afrika Korps landed at Tripoli in February 1941 to rescue Italian forces. Within weeks Rommel attacked, reversing Allied gains and beginning the see-saw North African campaign.

label: Arctic Convoys — UK to Murmansk
notes: 78 Allied convoys carried Lend-Lease supplies to Soviet Murmansk and Archangel, braving U-boats, German surface ships, and Arctic weather. Convoy PQ-17 (July 1942) was catastrophically scattered, losing 24 of 35 ships.

label: North Atlantic Convoy Routes — Mid-Ocean to UK
notes: Allied convoys crossing from North America used the MOMP (Mid-Ocean Meeting Point) where Canadian and British escorts exchanged convoy duty. The Battle of the Atlantic lasted the full duration of the war; U-boats sank 3,500+ Allied merchant ships.

label: Operation Market Garden — Ground Advance to Arnhem
notes: British XXX Corps (Guards Armoured Division) had to advance 100 km up a single road to relieve paratroopers at Arnhem. Delays and German resistance meant they reached only Nijmegen before Arnhem fell.

label: Operation Bagration — Minsk to Warsaw
notes: Soviet summer offensive destroyed Army Group Centre and advanced 600 km, from Minsk to Warsaw, in two months. Greatest single Allied victory of the war by territory gained.

label: Soviet Advance into Eastern Europe — 1944
notes: Soviet forces drove westward through Romania, Bulgaria, Hungary, and into Austria, bringing Eastern Europe under Soviet control and setting the stage for post-war division.

label: V-2 Ballistic Missile Attacks on London
notes: Germany launched 1,358 V-2 ballistic missiles against England (mostly London) from September 1944 to March 1945. 2,754 civilians died in London. The first ballistic missiles used in war.

label: V-1 Flying Bomb Campaign Against London
notes: Germany launched 10,000 V-1 flying bombs ('Doodlebugs') against England from June 1944. 6,184 civilians killed; 2,400+ aircraft and thousands of AA guns deployed to counter them.

label: Japanese Advance into China — Operation Ichi-Go
notes: Japan's largest single offensive in China (Operation Ichi-Go, April–December 1944) sought to destroy US air bases and open a land route to Southeast Asia. Over 500,000 Japanese troops defeated over 1 million Chinese Nationalist forces.

label: Operation Dragoon — Southern France Advance
notes: US 7th Army and Free French forces landed in southern France and raced up the Rhône Valley 750 km to Alsace in six weeks, liberating Marseille, Lyon, and Toulon en route.

label: Allied Spring Offensive — Rome to Po Valley
notes: Allied spring offensive (Operation Grapeshot) broke through German lines and crossed the Po Valley. German forces in Italy surrendered unconditionally on May 2, 1945.

label: Molotov–Ribbentrop Partition Line (1939)
notes: Germany and USSR partitioned Poland along the Bug River line after their joint invasion. Eastern Poland (100,000 sq km) was incorporated into Soviet Ukraine and Belorussia.

label: Soviet Invasion of Finland — Karelian Isthmus
notes: Soviet forces attacked the Mannerheim Line on the Karelian Isthmus. Initial Soviet attacks were repulsed with heavy losses; breakthrough came only after massive reinforcements in February 1940.

label: British 8th Army — Messina to Monte Cassino
notes: British 8th Army crossed the Strait of Messina on September 3, 1943, advancing up the Adriatic coast and fighting at the Gustav Line through the winter of 1943–44.

label: Japanese Invasion of the Philippines
notes: Japan invaded the Philippines simultaneously with Pearl Harbor. General MacArthur's forces retreated to Bataan Peninsula; fell April 1942. MacArthur famously departed leaving the garrison to face the Bataan Death March.

label: Persian Corridor — Lend-Lease to USSR
notes: The Persian Corridor (via Iran) delivered 5 million tons of Lend-Lease supplies to the USSR, accounting for 23% of total US aid. Britain and USSR jointly occupied Iran to secure this route.

label: Elbe Day — US and Soviet Forces Link Up
notes: US 69th Infantry Division and Soviet 58th Guards Division met at Torgau on the Elbe River on April 25, 1945, cutting Germany in two. Celebrated as 'Elbe Day' and a symbol of Allied unity.

label: German Invasion of Norway — Oslo to Trondheim
notes: Germany landed troops simultaneously at six major Norwegian ports including Oslo, Bergen, Trondheim, Narvik, and Stavanger in Operation Weserübung — the first joint amphibious, airborne, and naval operation in history.

label: Slim's 14th Army — Burma Reconquest
notes: General Slim's British 14th Army crossed the Irrawaddy River and fought the battle of Meiktila (March 1945), defeating Japan's Burma Area Army and recapturing Rangoon by May 3, 1945.

label: MacArthur — New Guinea Advance
notes: MacArthur's forces advanced along the northern coast of New Guinea over 2,400 km using amphibious leapfrogging tactics, bypassing Japanese strongpoints to advance toward the Philippines.

label: Soviet Strategic Counter-offensive — Moscow to Belorussia
notes: After Stalingrad and Kursk, Soviet forces pushed the German front westward 700 km in an 18-month strategic counter-offensive, liberating Kyiv (November 1943) and Minsk (July 1944).
